Article Title: OpenAI launches ChatGPT Images 2.0 with improved text rendering: Availability, price and more
Article Text:
ChatGPT Images 2.0 features

ChatGPT Images 2.0 availability and use cases





OpenAI has introduced ChatGPT Images 2.0, an updated version of its image generation model, ChatGPT Images. Announcing the new model, the company said: “ChatGPT Images 2.0 is the next step: a state-of-the-art model that can take on complex visual tasks and produce precise, immediately usable visuals”. OpenAI said that the new model is designed to handle complex image tasks and produce visuals that are more precise and usable. According to OpenAI, the new model brings major improvements in how it follows instructions and handles detailed elements in images. It can render small text, icons and user interface elements more accurately, which has been a challenge for earlier image models.“This model is a step change in detailed instruction following, placing and relating objects accurately, and rendering dense text, with the ability to generate across aspect ratios. Its sense of composition and visual taste means results feel less AI-generated and more intentionally designed. It’s accurate across languages and uses its expanded visual and world knowledge to fill in the gaps for you, so you get smarter images with less prompting,” OpenAI said in the official announcement.OpenAI said ChatGPT Images 2.0 also improves performance across languages. The model can generate text within images in languages such as Japanese, Korean, Chinese, Hindi and Bengali with higher accuracy.That includes not just translating a label or two, but generating visually coherent outputs where language is part of the design itself, from posters and explainers to diagrams and comics.This means users can create visuals like posters, diagrams and comics that include non-English text, with better readability and structure.The updated model introduces “thinking” features, which allows it to handle more complex tasks. When used with advanced modes, the model can search for real-time information, generate multiple images from a single prompt and check its own outputs.OpenAI said this helps users move from idea to final image more efficiently, especially for tasks that require accuracy and consistency.ChatGPT Images 2.0 supports a wider range of visual styles, including photorealistic images, illustrations, comics and cinematic visuals. It also allows users to generate images in different aspect ratios, making it suitable for formats such as social media posts, presentations and banners.The model can produce outputs that better match the requested style, with improvements in lighting, texture and composition.The new model is available to all users through ChatGPT, Codex and the API starting today. Advanced outputs with thinking are available to ChatGPT Plus,Pro, and Business users. The gpt-image-2 model is available in the API; pricing⁠ varies depending on the selected quality and resolution of the image.OpenAI said it can be used for tasks such as marketing design, educational content, storytelling and product development. Developers can also integrate the model into their own applications using the API, allowing for automated image generation and editing workflows.
Article Summary: OpenAI has introduced ChatGPT Images 2.0, an updated version of its image generation model. The new model is designed to handle complex image tasks and produce visuals that are more precise and usable. It can render small text, icons and user interface elements more accurately, which has been a challenge for earlier image models.

Article Title: A new divide has reportedly emerged among engineers within Google, with some allowed to use ...
Article Text:
Claude vs Gemini : Access gap inside Google



AI use tied to performance expectations at Google



Debate over AI adoption





A divide has reportedly emerged within Google as the company pushes wider adoption of artificial intelligence (AI) tools across its workforce. According to a Business Insider report, some employees, particularly within its DeepMind unit, have been allowed to use external AI tools for coding, while most others are restricted to internal systems. The situation has led to concerns among engineers about access, performance and fairness. The development comes at a time when Google is increasing expectations for employees to use AI in their daily work.According to the publication, some employees at Google DeepMind have been given access to Claude for coding tasks. At the same time, most engineers across Google are required to use the company’s own AI tools, including Gemini.The report said that this difference in access has created tensions internally. Some employees believe that Google’s internal tools are not as effective as Claude for coding, leading to frustration among teams that do not have access to external tools, it says.The divide comes as Google increases its focus on AI adoption across the company. According to the report, some engineers have been given specific AI-related goals that could impact their performance reviews.In certain cases, employees are expected not only to use AI for generating code but also to build tools that improve efficiency within their workflows.The report noted that Google generally restricts the use of external tools for several reasons. The company relies heavily on custom-built internal systems, and it also follows a “dogfooding” strategy, where employees use its own products to test and improve them before release.Other technology companies, however, allow more flexibility. For example, Meta has reportedly allowed employees to use external AI tools like Claude for internal work.The issue gained attention after a post by software engineer Steve Yegge, who claimed that Google’s internal AI adoption was lagging. He wrote: “The TL;DR is that Google engineering appears to have the same AI adoption footprint as John Deere, the tractor company.”The comment drew a response from DeepMind CEO Demis Hassabis, who said: “Maybe tell your buddy to do some actual work and to stop spreading absolute nonsense. This post is completely false and just pure clickbait.”
Article Summary: Some employees at Google DeepMind have been given access to Claude for coding tasks. Most engineers across Google are required to use the company’s own AI tools, including Gemini. The divide comes as Google increases its focus on AI adoption across the company.

Article Title: Meta CTO Andrew Bosworth: When I start to experience the stress, that's a ...
Article Text:
Meta CTO shared coping strategies for stress

Meta CTO to engineers looking to join technology industry

Poll What is your primary method for coping with stress? Exercise Meditation Talking to friends/family





Meta’s chief technology officer Andrew Bosworth, who oversees the company’s Reality Labs division, says that stress is not something he encounters often, but when it surface, he treats it as a signal to reprioritise. According to a report by Business Insider, speaking during an Instagram ‘ask-me-anything’ session, Bosworth explained that he usually feels stressed only four or five times a year, and the trigger is almost always the same: being over-scheduled. Bosworth added that when he starts to feel stress, it’s mainly because he worries he won’t have enough tome for the ‘important work’. He described busyness as the root cause, noting: “When I start to experience the stress, that’s a useful signal for me. What is the important work that, if you dedicated the time to it, you would be okay? And, how do we reprioritise the urgent stuff that’s being noisy?”Bosworth also talked about some coping mechanisms which help to get out of a stressful situation. He explained that in order to manage stress he relies on conventional methods such as deep breathing, exercise, and spending time with his wife and kids. He also talks about stress opening, often with his wide, which he says helps him to reset. While he doesn’t consider his strategies unique, he emphasised that they are effective in keeping him grounded.Executives across industries use different approaches to cope with high-pressure roles.Some CEOs practice yoga, take long walks, or spend time in nature. Meta’s CEO Mark Zuckerberg previously told podcaster Joe Rogan that waking up and checking his phone felt like being “punched in the stomach,” and he manages stress by exercising in the mornings.Recently, Meta’s chief technology officer Andrew Bosworth had a simple answer when a student asked how to enter the tech industry: stop waiting and start building. During an Instagram “Ask Me Anything” session this week, Bosworth was asked by a college freshman what it takes to break into technology. His response was direct and practical — focus less on theory and more on doing. “You just have to immerse yourself in it,” Bosworth said. “You should just constantly be building.”“That's what's going to give you the best chance of having the relevant skill set that is needed to make a difference in technology,” he said.
Article Summary: Andrew Bosworth, Meta’s chief technology officer, says stress is not something he encounters often, but when it surface, he treats it as a signal to reprioritise. The trigger is almost always the same: being over-scheduled. Bosworth also talks about stress opening, often with his wide, which he says helps to reset.

Article Title: John Ternus: Who is John Ternus, Tim Cook’s successor, and Apple’s new CEO
Article Text:
John Ternus Apple CEO

John Ternus’ early career and rise within Apple

What challenges await Apple's next CEO

Poll Which product innovation do you think had the biggest impact? iPad AirPods iPhone MacBook Neo





Apple has announced that John Ternus will succeed Tim Cook to become the new CEO on September 1, 2026. Cook, who has led Apple for 15 years, will now transition to the role of executive chairman for the board. Ternus, who is Apple’s senior vice president of hardware engineering, has been a central figure in shaping the company’s most iconic products and is now been tasked with steering Apple into its next era of innovation. The transition, which was approved unanimously by the Board of Directors, follows a thoughtful, long-term succession planning process. Cook will continue in his role as CEO through the summer as he works closely with Ternus on a smooth transition. As executive chairman, Cook will assist with certain aspects of the company, including engaging with policymakers around the world.John Ternus joined Apple in 2001 as part of the product design team and became a vice president of Hardware Engineering in 2013. He brought with him a strong background in mechanical engineering. He earned his Bachelor’s degree in Mechanical Engineering from the University of Pennsylvania and started his career at Virtual Research Systems, where he worked on advanced virtual reality technologies.Throughout his tenure at Apple, Ternus has overseen hardware engineering work on a variety of groundbreaking products across every category. He was instrumental in the introduction of multiple new product lines, including iPad and AirPods, as well as many generations of products across iPhone, Mac, and Apple Watch. Ternus’s work on Mac has helped the category become more powerful and more popular globally than at any time in its 40-year history. That includes the recent introduction of MacBook Neo, an all-new laptop that makes the Mac experience even more accessible to more people around the world.This past fall, his team’s efforts were on full display with the introduction of a redefined iPhone lineup, including the incredibly powerful iPhone 17 Pro and Pro Max, the radically thin and durable iPhone Air, and the iPhone 17, which has been an incredible upgrade for users.With John Ternus as the new CEO, Apple gets a hardware leader at a pivotal moment. While Apple has seen tremendous success with iPhones and products like AirPods, the company has struggled in areas such as mixed reality, generative AI, the smart home and autonomous vehicles, according to Bloomberg.Apple has been spending significantly less on AI investments compared to rivals Meta, Amazon, Alphabet, and Microsoft. The company's AI-enabled Siri, originally slated for 2025, has been delayed until 2026 or later due to technical challenges. Apple has also lost several senior AI team members since January, many of whom have joined Meta's AI and Superintelligence Labs during talent wars this year.Despite these concerns, Apple is expecting strong end-of-year sales, with year-on-year revenue growth of 10% to 12% for its holiday quarter ending in December, fueled by the iPhone 17 model release. The company's shares are trading close to an all-time high following strong October earnings, though its roughly 12% stock price increase this year lags behind Big Tech rivals Alphabet, Nvidia and Microsoft.The reshuffle extends past the corner office. Johny Srouji, Apple's silicon architect, has been promoted to chief hardware officer, absorbing Ternus's old portfolio. Tom Marieb takes over day-to-day hardware engineering and reports to Srouji. Ternus joins Apple's board on September 1. Arthur Levinson, the company's non-executive chairman for the past 15 years, becomes lead independent director on the same day.
Article Summary: John Ternus will succeed Tim Cook to become the new CEO on September 1, 2026. Cook, who has led Apple for 15 years, will now transition to the role of executive chairman for the board. The transition follows a thoughtful, long-term succession planning process.

Article Title: Sam Altman: Quote of the day by Sam Altman: “I expect some really bad stuff to happen because of the technology... Very soon the world is going to have to contend with incredible video models that can
Article Text:
Quote of the day by Sam Altman

Quote of the day by OpenAI CEO Sam Altman



“I expect some really bad stuff to happen because of the technology... Very soon the world is going to have to contend with incredible video models that can deepfake anyone or kind of show anything you want.”



What the quote by Sam Altman highlights



Understanding deepfake technology



Why advanced video models are a concern



Impact on information and trust



Real-world risks associated with deepfakes



Efforts to manage and regulate AI risks



The role of public awareness



How AI continues to evolve



Other famous quotes by Sam Altman



“AI will probably most likely lead to the end of the world, but in the meantime, there’ll be great companies.”

“We are entering the age of intelligence.”

“The development of superhuman machine intelligence is probably the greatest threat to the continued existence of humanity.”

“Technology doesn’t solve problems by itself; people decide how it is used.”

A broader perspective on technology and responsibility



Final takeaway from Sam Altman's quote







People all over the world are talking more and more about the pros and cons of artificial intelligence as it continues to grow quickly. AI is a part of life now. It has things like writing, designing, and data analysis tools, as well as systems that can make videos and pictures. Experts are also worried about how these new technologies might be misused. Sam Altman , the CEO of OpenAI, is one person who has openly talked about the risks of very advanced AI systems. A lot of people are interested in what he said about deepfake technology and video models because they bring up a real and growing problem. It might be harder to tell what's real and what's fake as AI-generated content becomes more lifelike. This is a big deal for everyone, including people, businesses, and governments. Governments must urgently establish robust ethical frameworks to ensure safety while fostering beneficial AI innovation.This statement brings up a specific worry: the wrong use of AI to make videos. It says that the technology is new and powerful, but it can also be dangerous if you don't take the right safety precautions. Things that could hurt people, like lying, stealing someone's identity, or changing how people feel about something, are what "really bad stuff" means.The quote talks about how advanced AI systems can make videos that look real but might not show what really happened. This makes it harder for people to check facts and increases the chance of misunderstanding or abuse.Deepfake technology uses AI to change or make videos and audio look real. These systems learn how someone looks and talks by looking at a lot of pictures, videos, and voices. Once they have been trained, they can make new content that looks and acts a lot like the person they are copying.A deepfake video can make it look like someone did or said something they didn't really do. In the past, it was easy to find videos like these. But as AI models have gotten better, the quality has become much more realistic.The quote is about a worry that comes from how fast things are changing. It's getting harder for the average person to find fake content as technology gets better.AI can now make whole scenes instead of just edited clips, thanks to advanced video models. These systems can walk and talk like real people, take pictures that look real, and show feelings that look real.You can use these kinds of skills in a lot of different ways, like making movies, teaching, and simulating. You can also use the same tools to make content that is false or misleading.A fake but realistic video of a famous person could go viral on social media and change people's minds before it is proven to be false. People who know a lot about this say it's one of the most dangerous things. The quote's use of the phrase "show anything you want" shows how this could be used for bad things.Deepfake technology makes people less trusting, which is one of the worst things about it. A lot of people these days learn things by watching videos and looking at pictures. It's hard to know what's real when these sources are easy to change.This could change the way people talk to each other, how news is reported, and how evidence is used in court. If people start to doubt the truth of all digital content, they might not trust reliable sources as much.People are worried about more than just false information spreading; they're also worried about how it will change how society checks the truth in general.There are a lot of real-world risks that come with advanced deepfake technology. Identity theft is when someone uses someone's picture or voice without their permission. Misinformation is when false information is shared as true.Some people have used deepfake videos to spread false political messages or to pretend to be someone else in a money scam. These risks could get worse as the technology gets better if the right safety measures aren't taken.The quote shows that people know about these things happening in real life and need to be ready for them.Governments, tech companies, and research groups are all working to fix the problems that deepfakes and advanced AI systems are causing. Some of the work being done includes making rules about how to use these kinds of technologies and making tools that can find fake content.Some platforms are also putting labels or warnings on content made by AI to make it easier for people to understand what they are looking at. Researchers are also looking into ways to watermark content to show that it was made by AI.The goal of these steps is to stop people from using the technology for bad things while still letting people use it for good things.Another important part of keeping AI safe is making sure that the public knows about it. As technology changes, people need to know how it works and how it can be used for bad things.People can be more careful when they look at things online if they know about deepfake technology. One way to stop false information from spreading is to check multiple sources, make sure the information is correct, and be careful about sharing videos that haven't been checked.The quote indirectly shows how important it is to be aware by pointing out how real and powerful these technologies are becoming.Experts say that AI will keep getting better over the next few years. It looks like video generation models will get faster, more detailed, and easier to use.This progress will bring both good and bad things. It can make it easier to talk to people, learn, and be creative, on the other hand. It might also make the need for stronger protections and responsible use even more important.Sam Altman's statement shows how to find a middle ground between being careful and trying new things.People are talking about deepfakes as part of a bigger conversation about how society handles powerful technologies. New things that people make have always had both good and bad effects. The most important thing has always been how they are used and controlled.The rapid growth of AI makes this process more difficult. People who work for the government, businesses, researchers, and users all need to work together.The quote reminds us that new ideas can help us move forward, but we need to plan and use them carefully to avoid problems.Sam Altman's statement brings up a very important point about the digital world we live in today: the growing power of AI-generated content and the risks that come with it. The quote talks about how things can be misused, which makes people more aware of their own safety, the rules, and safety in general.As AI gets better, it's important to know both the good and bad things about it. It is now possible to make realistic digital content, which opens up new possibilities. However, systems are also needed to make sure that the content is correct, reliable, and used properly.
Article Summary: OpenAI CEO Sam Altman talks about the risks of deepfake technology and video models. He says the technology is new and powerful, but it can also be dangerous if you don't take the right safety precautions. It might be harder to tell what's real and what's fake as AI-generated content becomes lifelike.

Article Title: Tesla owners are suing Elon Musk’s company and the reason is ‘promise’ made in 2017
Article Text:
Elon Musk with Tesla car

Tesla facing global backlash



The ‘Hardware 3’ problem in Teslas







Tesla is reportedly facing a growing wave of international lawsuits from angry Tesla car owners who claim they were sold a “self-driving” dream that has failed to materialise. The legal battle centers on ‘promises’ made as far back as 2017, with drivers across Europe, Australia and the US now demanding their money back, as per a report by Benzinga. The latest legal challenges arrive just two days before Tesla is set to report its first-quarter earnings.According to the report, the newest legal front opened last week in the Netherlands after a Dutch Tesla owner launched a massive campaign to organise European drivers who paid thousands of dollars for automated driving features they still cannot use in their Tesla cars.This joins a growing list of similar legal battles:An 80-year-old retired attorney in California paid $8,000 for “Full Self-Driving” (FSD) in 2017, and he recently won class-action status. He now represents roughly 3,000 owners seeking full refunds. Another lawsuit is reported to be in Australia where a major law firm is pursuing a separate class action, accusing Tesla of selling cars that are physically incapable of ever becoming fully autonomous.At the heart of these lawsuits is a technical bottleneck known as Hardware 3. This is the computer system installed in millions of Tesla vehicles produced over the last several years. The report notes that Wall Street analysts now say this hardware is likely too outdated to run Elon Musk ’s newest and most sophisticated AI software.Musk told investors in early 2025 that the company would eventually upgrade these older systems for “lifetime” buyers but admitted the process would be “painful and difficult.”In 2020-2021, Tesla offered free upgrades to some early buyers but in 2023, Tesla moved to “Generation 4” hardware which resulted in the owners who paid for “lifetime access” years ago find themselves stuck with obsolete equipment once again.
Article Summary: Legal battle centers on ‘promises’ made as far back as 2017, with drivers across Europe, Australia and the US now demanding their money back. Latest legal challenges arrive just two days before Tesla is set to report its first-quarter earnings.

Article Title: Jensen Huang may claim Nvidia chips can do "a whole bunch of applications" vs Google TPUs, but as Google AI Demis Hassabis says: A lot of people would like to run ...
Article Text:
Training vs. inference is the new battleground

Jensen Huang’s take vs. Demis Hassabis ’ argument

Poll Which company do you believe has the edge in AI chip technology? Google Nvidia Both are equal Neither, there are better options





The rivalry between tech titans Google and Nvidia is said to be heating up as the focus of the artificial intelligence (AI) boom shifts from teaching AI to actually using it to get answers quicker than ever. While Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang has claimed his chips (GPUs) are more versatile than Google’s, Google is preparing a major counter-move. At the Google Cloud Next conference this week, the search giant is expected to double down on its custom-made AI chips, known as Tensor Processing Units (TPUs), to meet a massive surge in demand, as per Bloomberg.In the world of AI, there are two main phases: Training (teaching a model like ChatGPT to learn) and Inference (where the AI actually answering your questions). While Nvidia’s chips are currently the “gold standard” for training, Google believes the future lies in specialised chips built specifically for inference that will allow the models to answer queries quickly.“It now becomes sensible to specialize chips more for training or more for inference workloads,” said Google Chief Scientist Jeff Dean, Bloomberg reported.This matters because more people are using AI daily, which means companies need cheaper and more efficient ways to run these models at scale.That’s why, companies like Google itself is using a mix of its TPUs and Nvidia GPUs.Nvidia’s Jensen Huang recently argued that his GPUs are superior because they can handle "a whole bunch of applications" that specialised TPUs simply cannot do. However, Google DeepMind CEO Demis Hassabis sees it differently. He noted that the world’s leading AI labs are increasingly desperate to get their hands on Google’s hardware.“A lot of people would like to run on both,” Hassabis said, highlighting that interest in TPUs has reached an all-time high.Google has a decade-long head start in designing its own chips. Analysts say this gives Google a “home-field advantage” as AI agents (programs that can perform complex tasks on a user's behalf) become the next big thing.“The battleground is shifting towards inference,” said Chirag Dekate, an analyst at Gartner. He noted that Google’s Gemini model is already among the fastest at complex reasoning, largely thanks to the infrastructure Google has built under the hood.Nvidia has already spent a reported $20 billion to bolster its own inference technology by acquiring Groq, that offers inference technology.Moreover, Google has previously touted inference capabilities for its chips. It also considered releasing separate chips for training and inference early on, according to Partha Ranganathan, a vice-president and engineering fellow at Google.
Article Summary: Google is expected to double down on its custom-made AI chips, known as Tensor Processing Units (TPUs), to meet a massive surge in demand. Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang has claimed his chips are more versatile than Google’s, but Google is preparing a counter-move.

Article Title: Marc Benioff shares 'stories' of Salesforce AI Agentforce 'helping' companies 'resolve issues' without any human interaction to prove that those saying software is dead are wrong
Article Text:
Salesforce chief executive Marc Benioff (Photo-AP)

The software bears are dead wrong.

At @Pearson, #Agentforce autonomously handles order statuses, refunds & lost access codes — increasing the percentage of customer questions resolved without any human interaction by 40%, says VP Gabriele Bauman.

At @PenFed, our

Agentforce

agent handles password resets & account unlocks for employees — reducing total IT tickets by 40%, says EVP & CIO Shree Reddy. This is the Agentic Enterprise delivering real ROI today — not hype. Agenticware, not Software.

Benioff on why ‘SaaSpocalypse’ won’t happen







Salesforce CEO Marc Benioff has a message for everyone who thinks his company is in trouble: you are looking at this all wrong. The comment comes after the software giants watched their stocks fall following ‘fears’ that artificial intelligence (AI) will make traditional software companies obsolete. If AI agents can do the work that companies currently hire armies of employees to do, why would those companies keep paying for expensive software sold on a per-employee basis. Benioff is answering it directly, and to make his case, he has turned to real-world customer stories.According to a report by The Wall Street Journal, and a social media post on X (formerly Twitter), its customers have seen 40% fewer human interactions and 40% fewer IT tickets. Here’s his full post X:Benioff explains that its agents now autonomously handle queries about order statuses, refunds and lost access codes for its customers, and another agent is helping a company employees reset passwords and unlock accounts.Benioff’s argument rests on multiple pillars. First, the leading AI labs cannot simply replace what Salesforce offers, which includes decades of experience in data security, regulatory compliance and industry-specific workflows. He says these cannot be replicated overnight by a general-purpose AI model.The second argument is that customers who might theoretically try to build their own AI-powered alternatives would run serious risks around brand safety and compliance that make Salesforce's established platform far more attractive.Finally, Benioff says that the AI labs themselves see Salesforce as a partner, not a rival. Both Anthropic and OpenAI use Salesforce's workplace app Slack, and Salesforce has integrated their models into Agentforce.Salesforce launched Agentforce in late 2024, allowing businesses to build custom autonomous agents for use in their own workflows. Early reviews were mixed. Salesforce responded by building a new data layer into its technology stack.
Article Summary: Salesforce launched Agentforce in late 2024, allowing businesses to build custom autonomous agents for use in their own workflows. Salesforce CEO Marc Benioff has a message for everyone who thinks his company is in trouble: you are looking at this all wrong.

Article Title: Evan Spiegel: Snapchat CEO warns Ray Ban maker on Meta smartglasses partnership; says: Mark Zuckerberg's Meta Glasses are harmful for your brand, as …
Article Text:
Representative Image

“It’s much more harmful for [Essilor]Luxottica than it is for Meta. I think Meta needed to partner with Luxottica because the Meta brand is not something that people want to put anywhere near their face. I think that Meta really needed [the partnership],”

“What’s challenging for Luxottica is that they took like the most iconic, crazy-high-margin product and they destroyed the margin and then they associated with Meta. We’ll see if that pans out over time, if that was the right brand choice for them, but I can definitely see why Meta needs to camouflage their brand, which I think a lot of people don’t resonate with and don’t like,”

Concerns around Meta’s smart glasses and how Snap is trying to gain traction in this space







Snap CEO Evan Spiegel has raised concerns about Facebook-parent Meta’s partnership with EssilorLuxottica, the company behind Ray-Ban glasses. The chief executive of the messaging app has suggested that the collaboration could benefit Meta more than it affects the eyewear brand. Speaking in a recent interview with YouTuber David Senra, Spiegel criticised Meta’s brand perception in the smart glasses segment and questioned the long-term impact of its association. His remarks come as Meta continues to expand its presence in wearable technology through products like the Ray-Ban Meta AI glasses, developed in partnership with EssilorLuxottica.Spiegel added. He added that the collaboration could carry risks for the eyewear company’s brand positioning and pricing strategy.Spiegel noted.Spiegel’s statements reflect some of the challenges Meta faces in its strategic positioning in the smart glasses space.Meta has had to confront several privacy-related issues, especially regarding the handling of user data and its plans to incorporate features such as facial recognition.At the same time, Meta has relied on the Ray-Ban name to anchor its smart glasses offerings, with branding often emphasising the eyewear label over Meta’s own identity. This approach highlights the role of established consumer brands in building trust in emerging product categories.However, despite Spiegel's criticism of Snapchat , the company continues to struggle with the popularity of its own hardware product, Spectacles, an augmented-reality eyewear. Although Spectacles have been around for a few years now, the company is still targeting developers and creatives.The broader smart glasses market is also becoming increasingly competitive, with companies such as Google, Samsung, and Apple expected to expand their presence. Meta has reported steady sales of its smart glasses so far, but the long-term impact of its partnership with EssilorLuxottica remains uncertain.Spiegel’s remarks add to the ongoing debate over branding, privacy, and user acceptance of wearable technology as companies continue to explore how to bring smart glasses into wider use.
Article Summary: Snap CEO Evan Spiegel has raised concerns about Meta’s partnership with EssilorLuxottica, the company behind Ray-Ban glasses. He suggested that the collaboration could benefit Meta more than it affects the eyewear brand. His remarks come as Meta continues to expand its presence in wearable technology.

Article Title: Mira Murati: After months of launching 'almost full-scale raid' on OpenAI ex-CTO Mira Murati’s Thinking Labs, Mark Zuckerberg's Meta poaches yet other staff from $12 billion startup
Article Text:
Mira Murati

Mark Zuckerberg ’s ‘full scale raid’ on Thinking Labs

Meta hired fifth founding member from Mira Murati's Thinking Machines Lab





Mark Zuckerberg-led social media giant Meta’s hiring push in the AI space continues, as the company has reportedly hired three more employees from Thinking Machines Lab, the $12 billion startup founded by former OpenAI Chief Technology Officer (CTO) Mira Murati. The latest move comes after months of aggressive recruitment efforts by Meta, which has been targeting top talent from rival AI companies. According to a Business Insider report, Mark Jen and Yinghai Lu, both part of the startup’s founding team, have left Thinking Machines Lab and joined Meta. Jen is a software engineer who previously worked at Meta, while Lu has previous experience at both Meta and OpenAI and focuses on AI inference, which helps improve how chatbots reason.A third employee, Tianyi Zhang, an AI researcher known for widely cited academic work, has also moved to Meta, the report says.The latest hires add to a growing list of employees Meta has recruited from Mira Murati’s startup. As per previous report, Meta has already hired several members of the founding team, including Joshua Gross earlier this month. Gross worked on the company’s main product.Last year in August, Meta tried to buy OpenAI’s former Chief Technology Officer (CTO) Mira Murati startup, Thinking Machines Lab. But as she declined to sell her company despite a $1 billion offer, Meta CEO Mark Zuckerberg responded by launching a “full-scale raid” on the startup.Earlier this month, Meta hired veteran software engineer Joshua Gross, who helped build and ship Thinking Machines Lab’s flagship product Tinker from “zero-to-one”. As per his LinkedIn profile, Gross leads the engineering teams at Meta Superintelligence Labs. He had earlier worked at both OpenAI and Meta before cofounding Murati’s startup.Thinking Machines Lab, launched last year, quickly became a major hub for AI talent after raising $2 billion at a $12 billion valuation. The company has expanded its team rapidly, with its workforce growing to around 130 people.At the same time, it has faced strong hiring pressure from competitors. OpenAI has also recruited from the startup, while Thinking Machines Lab has brought in well-known figures such as Soumith Chintala, creator of PyTorch, as its CTO.
Article Summary: Mark Zuckerberg-led social media giant Meta’s hiring push in the AI space continues. The company has reportedly hired three more employees from Thinking Machines Lab, the $12 billion startup founded by former OpenAI Chief Technology Officer (CTO) Mira Murati. The latest move comes after months of aggressive recruitment efforts by Meta, which has been targeting top talent from rival AI companies.

Article Title: Dyson launches Supersonic Travel hair dryer with compact design
Article Text: Dyson Supersonic Travel hair dryer is now official. Dyson has expanded its product lineup with the launch of Supersonic Travel hair dryer which is a compact styling tool engineered specifically for modern travellers. Designed with global voltage compatibility, the new dryer delivers Dyson’s signature fast styling and hair protection in a portable form factor, ensuring smoother and shinier hair wherever you go. 32% smaller and 25% percent lighter than Dyson’s full-sized Supersonic hair dryer, the new hair dryer is carry-on compatible, fitting easily into handbags, carry-ons and cabin luggage.Founder and chief engineer James Dyson emphasised that the Supersonic Travel is not just a smaller version of the original: “We didn’t just shrink a dryer; we re-engineered it for travel. The Supersonic Travel delivers high-performance styling while protecting hair from extreme heat.”Despite its compact size, the Supersonic Travel delivers the same effortless drying performance Dyson is known for. It features intelligent heat control technology that measures airflow temperature 100 times per second, preventing extreme heat damage. The airflow is precisely engineered to dry hair quickly and evenly, keeping it healthy and glossy.Another standout feature is attachment compatibility. Travellers can use their existing Dyson Supersonic or Supersonic Nural attachments with the new model, ensuring styling flexibility on the go.Since the launch of the original Supersonic in 2016, Dyson has redefined hair dryer design by placing the high-speed motor in the handle for better balance and introducing intelligent heat control to protect natural shine. The Supersonic Nural, launched earlier, added scalp protection features. The new travel edition extends this legacy, bringing Dyson’s core technologies into a smaller, lighter form factor without compromising results.The Dyson Supersonic Travel hair dryer is being introduced in select markets, with launch details for India to be announced closer to availability.
Article Summary: The Dyson Supersonic Travel hair dryer is 32% smaller and 25% lighter than the full-sized model. It is carry-on compatible, fitting easily into handbags, carry-ons and cabin luggage. It features intelligent heat control technology that measures airflow temperature 100 times per second, preventing extreme heat damage.

Article Title: Adobe unveils CX Enterprise for the agentic AI era at Adobe Summit 2026
Article Text:
Adobe AI summit

Price and availability



Adobe CX Enterprise features







At the Adobe Summit conference, Adobe announced the launch of CX Enterprise, a new end-to-end agentic AI system designed to redefine customer experience orchestration (CXO). The platform integrates AI agents, agent skills, and Model Context Protocol (MCP) endpoints with an intelligence and governance layer, enabling businesses to deliver reliable, auditable, and highly personalized brand experiences at scale.Adobe positioned CX Enterprise as the next step in modern marketing, moving beyond isolated AI use cases into fully agentic enterprises. The system is powered by Adobe Brand Intelligence, a continuously learning reasoning engine that captures evolving brand signals, and Adobe Engagement Intelligence, a decisioning engine optimized for customer lifetime value. Together, they allow businesses to orchestrate personalized customer journeys while ensuring compliance with brand guidelines.While Adobe has not disclosed specific pricing, CX Enterprise will be available globally as part of Adobe’s enterprise suite. It will integrate seamlessly with Adobe Experience Platform (AEP), which already powers over one trillion customer experiences annually, serving as the contextual layer for CX Enterprise agents.* New AI Agents: Streamline tasks across customer engagement, content supply chains, and brand visibility.Integrated natively into Adobe solutions and extended to partner platforms like Amazon Quick, Anthropic Claude Enterprise, ChatGPT Enterprise, Gemini Enterprise, IBM watsonx Orchestrate, and Microsoft 365 Copilot.* Agent Skills Catalog: Reusable instructions packaged for workflows such as performance reviews, content creation, and journey orchestration. Businesses can customize skills with additional context to reflect organizational needs.* Developer Tools: Access to agentic skills, MCP servers, and infrastructure to build custom use cases. Designed for interoperability with Anthropic, Google Cloud, Microsoft, OpenAI, and more.* CX Enterprise Coworker: A coordination layer that translates business goals into multi-step actions. For example, it can assemble audience segments, creative assets, and performance insights to execute a targeted campaign aimed at boosting cross-sell performance.Adobe emphasized its commitment to an open ecosystem, announcing expanded partnerships with AWS, Anthropic, Google Cloud, IBM, Microsoft, NVIDIA, and OpenAI.
Article Summary: Adobe announced the launch of CX Enterprise, a new end-to-end agentic AI system. The platform integrates AI agents, agent skills, and Model Context Protocol (MCP) endpoints with an intelligence and governance layer. The system is powered by Adobe Brand Intelligence and Adobe Engagement Intelligence.
